cycles.name: Foundation Forge
focuses: Build baseline movement quality across squat, split squat, and hinge patterns.
Begin shoulder stabilisation work and establish core anti-rotation capacity.

cycles.name: Structural Strength
focuses: Tempo-based strength in squat and RDL with shoulder-friendly pressing.
Deepen shoulder rehab integration and introduce rotational power for padel transfer.

paragraphs: Cycle 1 built the platform. This cycle is where we start loading it. You'll see the same patterns as last month — squat, hinge, press, pull — but now under controlled tempo with real intent.
Mentally, focus on owning the eccentric on every rep and keeping shoulder rehab as important as the strength work, not second to it. Earn the tempo. The weight will follow.
outcomes: Noticeably stronger Back Squat and Barbell RDL under tempo
Shoulder handles pressing without irritation or pinching
First exposure to real rotational power through med ball work
Cleaner landing mechanics on single-leg plyometrics
Improved anti-rotation core control under load

cycles.name: Rotational Engine
focuses: Convert strength into padel-specific rotational power through progressive med ball and cable work.
Sharpen reactive landing mechanics and lateral court movement.

teaser.subtitle: You've pressed, pulled, and squatted under control. Now we turn strength into speed.
paragraphs: Cycle 3 is where the med ball stops being a warm-up and becomes the point of the session.
The court will feel different after this cycle — your shots will have a weight behind them you haven't felt before.
Earn it in these four weeks.

The MRI gave us a clear picture, and clear is good — we now train with the information instead of guessing. This cycle we do two things at once: we turn your shoulder into a stability project, building the muscles around the joint so they protect what the labrum can’t, and we go after your lower body honestly. No pressing this block, no rotational throws — that’s a deliberate call, not a setback.

Mentally, treat the shoulder rehab day with the same respect as a heavy squat. The small, slow, boring cuff work is the training right now — it’s what buys back your pressing and your serve later. Downstairs, we build your squat and hinge from real numbers and add weight every week you earn it.
By the end of eight weeks your shoulder should feel steady and controlled through its safe range, and your legs should be visibly stronger under the bar.
outcomes: A shoulder that stays calm and stable through all programmed movements
Back Squat load climbing weekly off a clean, grooved pattern
Stronger, more confident hip hinge (Barbell RDL) with no low-back rounding
Rotator cuff and scapular control that hold under fatigue
A real strength baseline logged for the first time (Squat + RDL)
Cleaner single-leg balance and landing control for the court

cycles.name: Durability Build
focuses: Bulletproof the shoulder and knees through eccentric overload and tendon-focused loading.
Integrate sport-load management — training that coexists with harder padel weeks.

teaser.subtitle: You’re spending this block making the shoulder bulletproof and your legs strong. That’s the price of admission for what comes next.
paragraphs: When the joint is ready, we turn this foundation into real power — the kind that shows up in your shot, not just the gym.
Protect it now. Unleash it soon.

cycles.name: Integrated Athlete
focuses: Fuse strength, power, and conditioning into one robust athletic profile.
Establish an autoregulated maintenance rhythm for long-term use.

days.completionMessage: Strong squat work today, Sina — that’s the load that builds your base. Refuel and hydrate.

cards.title: The Shoulder Rule This Cycle
cards.body: Your MRI showed a 30% tear in the front of the labrum. We’re managing it conservatively — no surgery, no panic, just smart loading. That means zero pressing this cycle and no rotational throws, on purpose. The cuff and scapular work on Day 4 is the most important training you’ll do all week. Treat it like it matters, because it’s what protects the joint and earns your pressing back.

cards.title: Your Pain Traffic-Light
cards.body: Learn this and use it every session. A dull ache 0–3 out of 10 during shoulder work is fine — that’s tissue being asked to work. A sharp or pinching pain at the front of the shoulder, or any ache that lingers more than 24 hours, is a red light: stop that movement immediately and message me. Never push through a sharp catch to finish a set. Honesty here is what keeps you off the operating table.

cards.title: Build the Squat From Reality
cards.body: You’re squatting around 30–40 kg, and that’s our honest starting point — no ego, no rush. We’re using moderate reps at a controlled effort so you groove the pattern and your body adds load safely. Your job is clean, full-range reps; my job is adding weight each week you earn it. Strength here comes from consistency, not from grinding heavy too soon.

cards.title: Log Your First Real Baseline
cards.body: We have no recorded numbers from your first two cycles, so week one we fix that. Log your weight and RPE (effort 1–10) on Back Squat and Barbell RDL every session. This is the data that lets me progress you precisely instead of guessing. Two numbers, every session — that’s the whole ask.

cards.title: Sleep Is Where the Shoulder Heals
cards.body: Cuff and tendon tissue remodel during deep sleep — this is not a throwaway line, it’s mechanism. Aim for 7–9 hours, and protect the night before Day 4 especially. Under-sleep this cycle and the shoulder progress is the first thing to stall. You can do everything right in the gym and still spin your wheels if recovery is an afterthought.

cards.title: Padel On Off Days — Keep It Light
cards.body: While the shoulder is healing, keep court time technical and moderate — no maximum-effort matches or big serving sessions. Hard padel stacks rotational and overhead load right onto the joint we’re protecting, and it will slow your progress, not speed it. Think footwork, placement, controlled rallies. The power comes back later, once the foundation is solid.

cards.title: Fuel the Repair
cards.body: Tissue repair needs raw materials — protein especially. Anchor each day around a solid protein source at every main meal and don’t train this cycle underfed. I haven’t built you a detailed nutrition plan yet, so treat this as the baseline we refine through our check-ins. Tell me what a normal eating day looks like for you and we’ll sharpen it.
